title: How the Internet Works
description: Pilot episode — approachable, visual-first explainer on Internet fundamentals. 7 scenes, ~90s at default durations.

data.title: How the Internet Works
data.subtitle: From your keyboard to the world
data.narration: Every time you open a website, send a message, or stream a video, a small miracle of engineering happens in milliseconds. Let's break it down.

data.heading: The Internet in 5 ideas

bullets.text: Packets — data is split into small chunks
IP addresses — every device has a unique number
Routers — traffic directors of the Internet
DNS — the phone book that translates names to IPs
TCP/IP — the rules that make it reliable

data.narration: The Internet is built on five simple ideas. Master these, and the rest falls into place.

data.heading: A request travels the world
data.caption: Each hop adds ~1-10ms. London to New York: ~80ms.
data.narration: When you type a URL, your request jumps through several hops before reaching its destination. Each hop is handled by a specialized piece of infrastructure.

data.heading: Packets: the postal system of data
data.body: Your browser doesn't send one big blob. It breaks data into packets of ~1500 bytes each — numbered, addressed, and sent independently. They may take different routes and arrive out of order. TCP reassembles them perfectly.
data.narration: Think of it like shipping a large painting by cutting it into puzzle pieces, mailing each separately, and reassembling on arrival. That's exactly what TCP/IP does with your data.

steps.explanation: We import Node's built-in DNS resolver — no library needed. DNS is so fundamental it's in the standard library.
We ask: 'What IP address belongs to google.com?' The OS queries a DNS server (usually your ISP's or Google's 8.8.8.8).
The result: an IP address. '142.250.185.46' is one of Google's IPs. Your request now knows where to go.

data.narration: DNS is the phone book of the Internet. It translates human-readable domain names into the IP addresses that routers actually understand. Let's see how simple it is to use.

data.heading: TCP: the reliable postman
data.body: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) guarantees delivery. It numbers packets, detects lost ones, and requests retransmission. The tradeoff: a bit of overhead. UDP skips that — used for games and video calls where speed > perfection.
data.narration: Reliability comes at a cost. TCP adds handshakes and acknowledgements — perfect for web pages. But when you're on a video call, a slightly garbled frame is better than a delayed one. That's why Zoom uses UDP, not TCP.

data.text: The Internet: simple ideas, extraordinary scale.
data.narration: Billions of devices. Millions of kilometers of fiber. Trillions of packets per day. All running on ideas from the 1970s — proof that great fundamentals last forever.
